description: Honey and many ripe fruits are 'kaelo', with the taste characteristic of sugar. Established sensory extensions include 'kaelo whinu' (sweet scent), and News from Nowhere uses 'kaelo haolu' for pleasing, flattering speech. These are comparisons with sweetness rather than a general word for goodness; 'welao' (good) carries an explicit positive judgement.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k' and release into open central 'a'. Keep the following true-mid front 'e' as its own stressed hiatus syllable. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for lateral 'l' and finish with rounded true-mid back 'o'.
usage_notes: Literal taste is the default. A named sensory channel or established expression makes an extension clear. The quality-noun rule supplies sweetness; 'ru kaelo haolu' is overly sweet or flattering speech.

semantic_domains.aesthetic: sweetness appreciated in flavour, scent, or an established sensory comparison
semantic_domains.physical: the taste associated with sugar, honey, and ripe fruit
